content.text: Test cases that faithfully reflect your production workload are the foundation of any useful eval. Synthetic inputs generated from imagination or templates rarely capture the ambiguity, noise, and domain-specific patterns your users actually send. Empirical, task-specific cases close that gap by grounding your eval in real evidence.
Why Empirical Cases Outperform Synthetic Ones

Synthetic test cases are fast to write but suffer from availability bias: you imagine the cases you already think the model handles, and miss the ones it silently gets wrong. Empirical cases, sourced from real user interactions, hold three structural advantages:
Realistic distribution.
The frequency with which edge cases appear in your corpus mirrors production, so eval failures predict production failures accurately.
Hidden vocabulary.
Users spell things oddly, abbreviate domain terms, and combine concepts unexpectedly. Real inputs capture this naturally.
Revealed failure modes.
Past bugs, user complaints, and A/B test losers are the highest-signal cases in any test suite — they are failures the model has already demonstrated.
A model can score 90% on a synthetic suite and 60% on real traffic. The difference is almost always the distribution of inputs, not the evaluation logic.
Building the Empirical Case Pipeline
The pipeline has five stages: collect production data, sample and stratify, augment with synthetic edge cases, label expected outputs, and version the resulting suite.
Stage 1 — Collect Production Inputs
Log every request your model handles in production. At minimum capture:
The raw user input (prompt, message, or document)
Any system-prompt context that was active
The model output (for later labeling)
Metadata: timestamp, session ID, user segment, language

If you cannot log for privacy reasons, use a synthetic-data technique only after you have profiled the real distribution — then generate inputs that match that profile rather than imagining them from scratch.

import anthropic, sqlite3, json
from datetime import datetime

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
conn = sqlite3.connect("eval_cases.db")
conn.execute("""
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS cases (
        id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
        input TEXT,
        system_prompt TEXT,
        output TEXT,
        expected TEXT,
        split TEXT,
        created_at TEXT
    )
""")

def log_interaction(input_text: str, system_prompt: str) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
    )
    output = response.content[0].text
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT INTO cases (id, input, system_prompt, output, split, created_at) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)",
        (response.id, input_text, system_prompt, output, "unlabeled", datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    )
    conn.commit()
    return output
Stage 2 — Sample and Stratify
Random sampling from a large log produces a useful but biased corpus: routine requests dominate and rare failure modes vanish. Stratified sampling corrects this.

Define your strata first — typical dimensions include input length (short/medium/long), topic or intent cluster, user segment (new/returning/power), and language. Then sample proportionally within each stratum, with one override: over-sample any stratum where you have observed failures.
Stage 3 — Augment with Synthetic Edge Cases

Empirical sampling alone leaves coverage gaps in rare-but-important situations: adversarial phrasing, maximum-length inputs, mixed languages, or inputs the system was never deployed against yet. Add a minority of deliberately constructed edge cases on top of your empirical sample.
Boundary inputs: minimum and maximum length, empty strings, Unicode edge cases
Conflicting signals: input contradicts the system prompt, two instructions clash
Adversarial phrasing: prompt injection attempts, jailbreak patterns, ambiguous imperatives
Rare domain vocabulary: technical terms, abbreviations, brand names
Format variations: markdown in a plain-text context, numbers as words vs digits
Keep augmented synthetic cases to roughly 20-30% of your total suite. A majority-synthetic suite loses its empirical grounding.
Stage 4 — Label Expected Outputs
Each case needs an expected output — or a rubric that defines what 'correct' looks like. Three labeling approaches suit different task types:
Task Type
Labeling Approach
Notes
Extraction / classification
Human labels exact answer
High agreement; cheapest to verify
Open-ended generation
Human provides rubric + exemplar
Score 1-5 on rubric axes
Code generation
Execute and verify test assertions
Fully automated; catches regressions precisely
RAG / factual Q&A
Human marks factual accuracy
Compare to source document, not memorized facts

For production logs you already have outputs, but those are model outputs — not gold labels. Labeling means judging whether the existing output was correct and, where it was not, writing the correct answer.
Achieving Meaningful Coverage
Coverage means exercising every distinct failure mode and usage pattern that matters, not simply reaching a case count. Think in slices, not raw numbers.
The 50/30/20 Slice Rule
A practical starting allocation:
Slice
Proportion
Purpose
Happy path
Typical inputs the model handles well; regressions surface here first
Edge cases
Boundary inputs, rare vocabulary, unusual formatting
Known failures
Past bugs and user complaints; prevents regression on addressed issues

Adjust as your system matures. A new deployment skews toward happy-path cases because you have few confirmed failures. A stable system should grow its known-failure slice faster — each production incident is a free regression test.
Input Diversity Checklist
Before finalizing a test suite, verify coverage across:
Length spectrum: at least 10% of cases at or near the context limit
Language: every language in your user base represents ≥5% of cases
Intent clusters: each major task type the model is asked to perform
Format variation: all input formats (markdown, HTML, plain text, structured data)
Ambiguity spectrum: some cases with clear answers, some that require inference
Sizing the Suite
Case count matters less than case quality, but quantity still sets your statistical floor for detecting regressions.
Stage
Recommended Size
Rationale
Initial launch
50–100 cases
Enough to detect gross failures; fast to label
Active development
200–400 cases
Detects ~5pp changes at 95% confidence
Stable production
500+ cases
Detects ~2pp changes; supports sub-group analysis
Per-stratum minimum
30 cases per slice
Below this, stratum statistics are unreliable

Grow your suite continuously. After each production incident, add the failing case before closing the bug. After each A/B test, add the losing examples to the permanent suite. A suite grown from failures converges on your true weakness profile faster than any other strategy.

def add_production_failure(
    input_text: str,
    system_prompt: str,
    bad_output: str,
    correct_output: str,
    failure_type: str,
    conn: "sqlite3.Connection"
) -> None:
    """Register a production failure as a permanent test case."""
    import uuid
    conn.execute(
        """INSERT INTO cases
               (id, input, system_prompt, output, expected, split, created_at)
           VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, 'test', ?)
        """,
        (str(uuid.uuid4()), input_text, system_prompt,
         bad_output, correct_output, __import__('datetime').datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    )
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT OR IGNORE INTO failure_tags (case_id, tag) VALUES (?, ?)",
        (input_text[:40], failure_type)
    )
    conn.commit()
Versioning and Reproducibility
A test suite that changes between runs produces uninterpretable trend data. Every mutation — adding, removing, or relabeling cases — must be tracked.
Hash the suite.
Compute a SHA-256 of the sorted case IDs and store it alongside every eval run. If the hash changed, the run is not directly comparable to the previous one.
Never delete cases.
Mark cases as 'retired' with a reason. Retirement history lets you audit why a metric improved — model got better, or hard cases were removed.
Tag cases with source.
Record whether each case came from production logs, synthetic augmentation, or a specific incident. Source tags enable slice-level analysis later.
Pin to a model version.
Labels assigned under one model's outputs can be systematically biased. When you retrain or switch models, re-audit a random 10% sample of labels.
Detecting Distribution Shift
A test suite calibrated on last month's traffic degrades in value as user behaviour shifts. Periodically compare your suite's input distribution to current production traffic.
Embed inputs with a lightweight model and cluster both suite and current traffic
If cluster centroids have drifted more than a threshold, sample new cases from the shifted clusters
Track vocabulary novelty: what fraction of words in current traffic never appear in your suite
Re-label a 5% sample every quarter to catch annotation drift (label standards change over time)

from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import numpy as np

def suite_coverage_score(suite_inputs: list[str], production_inputs: list[str]) -> float:
    """Cosine similarity between TF-IDF centroids. Below 0.85 indicates drift."""
    vec = TfidfVectorizer(max_features=2000)
    all_inputs = suite_inputs + production_inputs
    tfidf = vec.fit_transform(all_inputs)
    suite_centroid = tfidf[:len(suite_inputs)].mean(axis=0)
    prod_centroid  = tfidf[len(suite_inputs):].mean(axis=0)
    return float(cosine_similarity(suite_centroid, prod_centroid)[0, 0])

# score < 0.85 → add new samples from production
Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Symptom
Fix
Cherry-picking successes
Suite score far above user satisfaction
Sample randomly; do not hand-pick easy cases
Label leakage
Model trained on labeled cases; eval inflated
Keep labelers blind to model training data
Stale suite
Score climbs but complaints persist
Add distribution-shift check quarterly
Too few per stratum
Stratum scores vary wildly run-to-run
Minimum 30 cases per meaningful stratum
Gold label from model output
Eval measures self-consistency, not correctness
Human labels the first gold; model output is the candidate
Automating Case Collection
Manual log sampling is a reasonable starting point, but at scale you need automated pipelines that continuously harvest new cases, tag them, and surface them for labeling review.
Stream-Based Collection

Connect your model's inference endpoint to a stream processor that samples incoming requests according to a configurable policy. A reservoir-sampling approach ensures that rare inputs are not drowned out by high-frequency patterns:

import random, time
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict

@dataclass
class StreamCase:
    session_id: str
    input_text: str
    system_prompt: str
    output_text: str
    latency_ms: float
    timestamp: float
    sampled: bool = False

class ReservoirSampler:
    """Maintains a reservoir of N random samples from a stream."""
    def __init__(self, capacity: int = 500):
        self.reservoir: list[StreamCase] = []
        self.capacity = capacity
        self.seen = 0

    def add(self, case: StreamCase) -> bool:
        self.seen += 1
        if len(self.reservoir) < self.capacity:
            self.reservoir.append(case)
            case.sampled = True
            return True
        # Replace a random existing element with decreasing probability
        j = random.randint(0, self.seen - 1)
        if j < self.capacity:
            self.reservoir[j] = case
            case.sampled = True
            return True
        return False
Automated Failure Tagging
Not all cases are equally valuable. Automatically tag cases with signals that indicate elevated evaluation priority:
Latency outliers (>P95): slow responses often indicate complex or ambiguous inputs
User re-sends or edits: a follow-up 'no, I meant...' is a strong failure signal
Short outputs on open-ended tasks: may indicate refusal or failure to engage
High token count with low satisfaction score (if you collect ratings)
Requests that hit retry logic or returned an error
Writing High-Quality Expected Outputs
The quality of your expected outputs determines the ceiling of your eval's usefulness. A poorly-labeled case misleads the grader and produces noise instead of signal.
Criteria for a Good Gold Label
Minimal but sufficient.
The gold label captures everything the grader needs to verify correctness, and nothing extra. Verbose gold labels are harder to grade and introduce label drift as labelers update wording over time.
Criterion-anchored.
Every gold label should map to one or more of your pre-agreed success criteria. If the label cannot be traced to a criterion, the criterion is either missing or the case is out of scope.
Grader-compatible.

The gold label format must match what your grader expects. If the grader checks JSON structure, the gold label must be a valid JSON string. If the grader uses regex, make sure the gold does not contain characters that would confuse the pattern.
Multiple Valid Outputs
Many tasks have more than one correct answer. When you encode a single gold label, cases with valid-but-different outputs will score as failures. Use one of three strategies:
Strategy
When to Use
Implementation
Gold list
Small set of valid answers (< 10)
Store array; grader checks membership
Rubric label
Open generation with multiple valid forms
Label the rubric criteria, not the output text
LLM grader
Highly variable outputs (paraphrasing, summarization)
Judge scores against criteria rather than text matching
Integrating Cases into CI
A test suite only guards against regressions if it runs on every change. Wire your empirical suite into the continuous integration pipeline so that failures block deployment.

# eval_gate.py — run in CI before deploying a new model version
import sys, json, subprocess

THRESHOLD = 0.88  # minimum acceptable score on empirical suite

def run_eval(suite_path: str, model: str) -> float:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "run_eval.py", "--suite", suite_path, "--model", model],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    data = json.loads(result.stdout)
    return data["score"]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    model = sys.argv[1]
    score = run_eval("suites/empirical_v3.jsonl", model)
    print(f"Eval score: {score:.3f} (threshold: {THRESHOLD})")
    if score < THRESHOLD:
        print("FAIL: score below deployment threshold")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("PASS")
    sys.exit(0)

In your CI configuration, invoke this gate after building the model artifact but before any canary or production deployment. The gate should also emit the suite hash alongside the score so that any improvement can be traced to a model change, a suite change, or both.
Summary

Empirical, task-specific test cases are built from real production inputs, stratified to cover the distribution you care about, augmented with synthetic edge cases for gap-filling, and labeled with expected outputs before being versioned for reproducibility.
Start by logging real user inputs with metadata from day one
Stratify by length, topic, and user segment; over-sample known failures
Keep synthetic augmentation to 20-30% of the total suite
Grow continuously: every production incident adds a new regression test
Hash the suite on every run and treat distribution shift as a maintenance signal
